In regard to profits... I'd have to look at Sony/Bungie financials... but typically, when a large company buys a smaller one, they don't do so with cash on-hand.  There is some financial wizardry that goes on and often companies will bring in cash or investments - essentially take on debt - to make the purchase.  Often what will happen after the new company is folded in, that purchase debt is folded in against the value of the newly incorporated subsidiary.  Basically the debt the bigger company went into to buy the smaller company gets put on the books of the smaller company.  This is basically what is happening with the EA purchase by the Saudi investment firm right now.  I can't say this is what Sony specifically did with Bungie, but it is so common to do this these days that I wouldn't be surprised if some version of this occurred.  So what that means, essentially, is that from day 1 of Bungie as a Sony sub-company, it gets a 3.whatever billion dollar debt against its valuation.  Any money the company makes after that goes to pay down that debt.

There is also a consideration of valuation.  If Sony buys a multibillion dollar company, regardless of the debt involved, it gets to claim new assets in that value.  They say, "I paid this much, so this is the value of that asset."  It takes on debt, yes, but perhaps that debt is on Bungie's shoulders and Sony as a company has their asset value go up.  But if Bungie fails to pay down that debt, eventually that reflects negatively on their asset value.  This is how you can have a company that rakes in lots of money for Sony, but still gets a massive $700 million+ write down.  This is Sony saying that Bungie is currently worth $700 million+ dollars less on their balance sheet than the value they claimed when they bought them.  Again, I don't know the specifics of how Sony moves their money around, but assuming they DID saddle Bungie with the debt of their purchase, it indicates Bungie was still raking in money, but not enough to clear the entire pile.

Sony announcing the write-down was the nail in the coffin for Bungie, or Destiny specifically.  No voting board of investors is going to allow a company to make that kind of valuation loss without taking action.  The board doesn't care that the game is still getting played and still bringing in money.  All they see is a massive loss in value that needs correcting.

I played the heck out of Destiny 1.  I was super active in the first year of Destiny 2 before it became a dramatically different game.  When its end was first announced, I thought maybe it was time to tune back in and catch up on years of content I missed.  When I started researching what I could go back and do, because I genuinely couldn't remember what was out there, I found out that pretty much everything I remember of Destiny is from the first game.  And none of what I played in Destiny 2 is available to play... it's all gone.  In fact, as I looked into it more, almost NONE of Destiny 2 is playable right now.  I could point to hundreds of videos detailing what was lost.  What's insane about how Bungie went about the seasonal model, is at the end of a season, they essentially deleted the content.  Entire campaigns, planets, gameplay loops... all gone.  All that cost in time and effort to develop something that was deleted from the game after a season was over.

What this created was a system entirely dependent on FOMO.  Hooking someone and keeping them logging in every single day - day after day, month after month.  Depending entirely on THOSE players.  Discouraging everyone else.  Making it so a person can't miss a month... a season... a year... and jump right back into the game when they were ready without being clueless as to what was going on and totally unprepared for current content.  As I jump in now literal years after I last logged out, I have no idea what's going on.  At this point, I've played basically all the Destiny 2 content that is left and I have no idea who half the characters are.  Entire story arcs have happened for me off-screen.  The idea of having an essential story that just disappears is not a way to run a game.  At some point, you'll have a bad spell.  A DLC won't land with the players.  Or you'll just have fatigue.  Or someone just decides to play something else for a bit.  And once that happens, Destiny provides every incentive NOT to ever jump back in.

Bungie could have done this differently.  There are other 10+ year games that are still ticking that are continually adding content.  Destiny is the only one I know that removes what's been added.  Bungie could have done seasons in a way that kept the heart of seasonal updates... incentivized some level of FOMO but left the story content intact.  Give people a reason to jump back in after a year or more and start spending money again cuz they can catch back up.  Sure, they miss the limited time events.  They miss seasonal gear.  They miss some of those for-the-moment types of experiences.  But the rest of the game is there when a player is ready to come back and spend more money.  Bungie has noone to blame but themselves here.

"Because of the enormous scale of content that had to be produced nonstop, lest fans revolt against its scarcity." I think this is just fundamentally wrong and I think Monument of Triumph proves that this was just never the case. There will always be hardcore players that burn through your content in like a week, timegating quests and progress because of them pisses everyone off and left a sour taste in everyone's mouth. 

I genuinely believe that if we got a full vendor refresh, 2 dungeons, 5 new strikes, 3 new crucible maps, 1 gambit map, a raid, and a hardmode raid that isn't just spamming champions, once every year with fun new or old beloved seasonal events. Then Destiny would've been fine, sure you'd need some innovation every year, like onslaught or content like the coil. But I genuinely think that it would've been enough. You can't prevent players from leaving, but you can make sure they leave happy and willing to come back for something new.

You assess projects by setting a series of goals. Each Goal breaks down into parts. Goal Alpha breaks down into part 1, 2, and 3. Part 1 breaks down into Section A, B, and C. When I was making a 2D Bomberman Style adventure game, I started with the Character. The Character was broken down into Movement, HUD, and Bombs. Bombs got broken down into Bomb Placement, Bomb Activation, Bomb Damage, and Bomb Clean-Up. Smaller goals building up into larger goals.

Environment artist in the industry here. The biggest issue I face on a regular basis for time-sinks is what I call manager-bureaucracy. I get a task to make some modular assets - then I make the pieces in their block-out stage (grey simple shapes to gauge shape/size/silhouette/etc.). Those assets need to be approved by my Lead Environment artist. Once they're approved by the lead, they have to get approved by design/animation/lighting departments. Then they have to get approved by the Creative Director. Then they get kicked back to me and I get to model them at the highest quality bar - and the cycle repeats. Waves and waves of approvals and kickbacks and approvals just turn what should be a few days or a single week's worth of work turns into multiple weeks, or even months. There are too many managers with their hands in the cookie jar, and not enough people making the actual shit in the games. If the Creative Director's just had faith in me as an artist to make art, and not have to deal with all this manager-bureaucracy, shit would get made a hell of a lot faster.

if any hunters think wavesplitter looks fun after this, you could probably cook something simple but effective with fortune's favor or gyrfalcon's. just standard void hunter with void weapon build shenanigans, maybe even run suppressor grenades and echoes of domineering and instability (with a firepower mod on your gauntlets, of course) for some synergy with its intrinsic suppression there. keeping in mind you want lots of orbs of power.
if you're feeling creative, sixth coyote might help you get some extra orb generation, but i don't think it'd be that potent.
but honestly the most... interesting idea to me is actually Star-Eater Scales. We all know it lets you overcharge your super or whatever by picking up orbs, but what's more relevant here is that it gives more super energy when picking up orbs of power. Then, run Tablet of Ruin - gold from lead for extra heavy ammo (because why not), harsh refraction for more wavesplitter damage, you've got a few solid void synergy artifacts in there, but most importantly you get the combination of Elemental Supercharger and Elemental Siphon.
Siphon makes your void breaches give super energy and spawns more from your wavesplitter multikills, while Supercharger will give a large chunk of extra super energy when killing an Exhausted or Severed target with your wavesplitter... which, sounds like it won't happen often, until you realize that Wavesplitter intrinsically inflicts Exhaust because it stuns overload champions with its disruption effect (as does any weapon that is now listed as disrupting overload champions).

Point being, you could feed everything about your build from wavesplitter kills into rapidly accelerated super energy gains.

Something everyone fails to realize is the industry itself was growing back in the 90s and early 2000s.  There was no shortage of new tech for game dev and hardware to run the new tech.  Progress was happening at every level.  It was truly an exciting time to be a PC gamer.  Things have really stagnated in the last 10 years.  We dont get the innovation in hardware or tech that we had in the past.  So its much harder for a company to truly do something special.  Weve had like 30 years of development at this point with modern game engines and hardware, but its slowed down to a crawl.  It will take something truly innovative or ground breaking to make you feel what we did in the past.  And thats just the nature of the industry being so senior.

I grew up playing JRPGs during the SNES and PS1 era in the '90s. Outside of GoldenEye 007 on the N64, FPS games were never really my thing.

That changed in 2004 when I built my first gaming PC. I got completely hooked on shooters like DOOM 3, Far Cry, Unreal Tournament 2004, Painkiller, Half-Life 2, and so many others.

One day, I was at COMPUSA (the original Micro Center, lol) looking for something new to play when I came across the PC port of Halo: Combat Evolved. I had heard about Halo before, and Halo 2 had just launched on Xbox and was everywhere, so I figured I'd give it a shot.

I still remember it was a Friday morning because I had just cashed my paycheck before heading to COMPUSA. I got home, installed the game, and started the campaign.

It completely pulled me in.

I got so absorbed that I played right through the time I was supposed to leave for work. I actually called in sick just so I could keep playing. Before I even realized it, I had finished the entire campaign in one sitting. Around 1 a.m., I finally stopped, grabbed something to eat, and went to bed.

That was the first time I had ever beaten an entire campaign in one sitting—and the first time I ever called in sick because of a video game. That's how incredible Halo CE was.

It's honestly sad to see what happened to the studio over the years. But no matter what, I'll always cherish the amazing memories and unforgettable experiences their games gave me. I quit D2 3 years ago but now I am back grinding and playing all the Dungeons and Raids I missed.

So. As a hunter main that loves stasis. Everything about the build and the way people were trying to micromanage your gameplay is insane.
I've been using mask of fealty. My opinion, as someone getting a lot of value without the set bonus? He should've been running Grim Harvest + Winter's Shroud.
Shatterdive is NOT necessary, THAT is the throw. 
Grim Harvest alone is getting you max frost armor constantly, winter's shroud is giving you more DR and slows/freezes from the class ability that gives your melee back, it actually feeds into a consistent gameplay loop instead of randomly shatterdiving for frost armor instead of utilizing the exotic's shuriken-oriented shatter. Plus there's definitely better choices of weapons for synergy - what you had was fine but, why not bring a stasis primary that can help spawn stasis crystals? or Monte Carlo? but even like, a Headstone primary is going to spawn stasis crystals which is more stasis shards and thus melee energy, as well as opportunities to shatter crystals with your melee and tie into the build loop.
I swear, most hunters don't understand buildcrafting the way our Warlock and Titan - especially Warlock - bretheren do. 
Y'all need to take after the Bladedancers of lore with Blink and learn from the warlocks instead of just trash talking them. Like I did.
oh, and I stand by Glacier grenade being better than the new grenade for this build too. You can shatter the crystals with your shuriken and it makes a room clearing nuke.

edit: Another bonus point to Glacier Grenades that cross wasn't utilizing. You can use it as a healing grenade in a pinch. Spawn the wall, break it, stasis shards. Boom, healing on demand.

edit2: Grim harvest, Winter's Shroud, whispers of conduction/hunger/rime/fissures/refraction, go for high class/melee stats, glacier grenade, gambler's dodge, a primary with headstone or crystalline corpsebloom. 
Infinitely more reliable and consistent version of the same build. And far easier to use - you don't need to juggle 20 different moves like you're playing a fighting game trying to decide when to shatterdive, when to shatter with shurikens, when to shoot weapons instead. It just works. Like any good build should.
Everything you do except your dodge spawns stasis crystals. Shattering crystals with your shurikens spawns more shurikens and crystals. 
Your dodge gives you melee energy and DR on top of the frost armor (and if over 100, overshield too), your stasis shards heal you and give you melee energy back. 
Shattering any stasis crystals or frozen targets gives stasis shards, you get that frost armor from Grim Harvest without relying on awkwardly forcing Shatterdive in there - and it doesn't require a kill anymore, just shattering crystals!
It's just better. It just works. It's that simple. These hunters don't know what they're doing, and they're whining like babies because they don't know how to make their own build and blame Cross for it.
This version of the build has been MORE reliable for me than the build was for cross. And I'm not even using a set bonus.

Correction: Bungie was not Jason Jones' company, it was Alexander Seropian's (the other co-founder). Seropian made Bungie's first two games, Gnop! (a pong clone) and Operation: Desert Storm himself. He and Jones met in college in Chicago and Jones came onboard and they worked on Jones' project which became Minotaur, and then they worked on Pathways into Darkness together. They had lots of overlap, but Seropian was more the business side, Jones was more the technical side.

I first played Destiny 1 in its alpha. 

I was 13, and I saw a trailer with a titan patrolling the cosmodrome. He was wearing the helm of inmost light and using a red death, and after the short clip I saw the words “become legend”. 

I’m 26 today. I’ve been through a lot of change over the years, but the words “become legend” have never quite left my mind. 

I haven’t played as much in recent years, and neither have many of my friends as we’ve grown, but I’ve noticed that many of us come back to it when we’re struggling. I think it gives us a way to feel gratitude for the memories of simpler times. Maybe it gives us a chance at connecting with friends long lost. Maybe it allows our minds to be free and explore when the monotony of responsibility shows its true weight. Maybe it inspires me to “become legend” when I feel that spark waver.

I didn’t know it at the time, but the memories I made in this game would be some I will cherish for the rest of my life. 

One thing I can say for sure is that without belief, there is no Destiny. There is no legend to be created. Belief is the light that guides in the darkest night. We’ve shown our belief on June 9th, and we’ve continued to do so since. Eyes up guardians, because now is when we become legend.

"...there are other worlds than these."
After all this time, our journey as we know it, comes to an end. We killed the witness and brought balance to the universe...mostly. it's kind of fitting...because eventually, like all Guardians who meet and spend time together... eventually, we return to the wild. We seek other lands, challenges and worlds. We leave the safety of the tour, not because we want to, but because we feel compelled to do so. To go and find that loot. To find that secret. To defeat that boss, to conquer than raid and dungeon. We are the embodiment of the spirit of adventure and we owe it to this game and our younger selves that fell in love with it, to play until we have our fill and then, carry on. That we spread to other galaxies and slay gods and monsters and save the world again. I'm happy to have known you, Guardians. In every version of me, Destiny has been there. Through the most formative years of my life, we had Destiny. Adventure well, one and all, eat lots, dribk more and laugh louder. I wish you a full vault, heavy that never runs dry and ramen that never gets cold. Per audacia ad astra. Big love to you all. Stay safe out there Guardians, where you roam, eyes up.

Something nobody is talking about...it's not merely the update that is at the core of Destiny 2's resurgence. It's that it's no longer being updated. My wife was a huge D1 and D2 fan with thousands of hours in both. What drove her away?  The same thing that drove her away from Warframe and many other games...live service. Now that Destiny 2 isn't constantly being updated with game changing 'fixes' and gear and builds that we love suddenly being nerfed, she came back and now we're both playing the heck out of it!

This is what happens when the update is made with deliberate, player friendly choices by passionate developers and not designed by committee.

This is what happens when the business side gives free reign to the creative team, since they no longer see d2 as a cash cow.

This is what we had hoped Bungie would do when they went independent from activision, and the real tragedy is that it took them this long, and the "death" of the Destiny IP to do so.

13:00 To clear up a common misconception, Witherhoard does not make enemies taken, it makes them blighted. Malfeasance and Wish-ender have a specific interaction with Witherhoard blighted enemies, we know these effects are separate from being "taken" because Wish-Ender has a 10% increase to taken enemies, and 25% increase to Blighted enemies. Witherhoard and 1k do however interact with each other. A Witherhoard blighted enemy will take 100% increased tick damage from the 22 additional DoT ticks 1k applies.

For us Warlocks, I highly recommend trying Sanguine Alchemy and Well (if you have a Well or don't want to use it, prismatic with a Song of Flame and healing rift). The 4x weapon surge on top of the debuff from marking the boss makes it do SO much damage. Boots of the Assembler is also a great option for your team, keeping everyone alive and your abilities up when running Ember of Benevolence, then providing a decent damage boost for you and your allies during DPS in the Well.

If you're running prismatic you can activate Transcendence for the weapon buff, throw a grenade for weaken and shatter, and an arcane needle for unravel (Horde Shuttle anyone?). The aspects for prismatic are really flexible too, and you can easily use whatever. I like running Helion + Weaver's Call because I'll be using my rift a lot and they increase my damage a bit, but substituting either for Devour is really good too. Also, Facet of Hope (or Ember of Singeing for Solar users) is very important to giving you rifts back to back without having to worry about investing into Class ability stat.

Sanguine users, running a single solar scavenger on your boots (since you don't have to worry about surges now) gives you two ammo instead of one per heavy brick, which will effectively double the amount of ammo you find on the ground. Pairing this with a high, preferably max, weapon stat, and with Gold from Lead in the artifact, you will almost NEVER go into a DPS phase without full ammo. I even used this set up messing around on Ultimate level content, and I could use it to ad clear large crowds or nuke champions without a worry in the world (1K also has 100 ammo gen with the catalyst, TONS of ammo).

Song of flame reduces damage, it applies first tick of scorch and ignition is counted as being from the ultimate. With 200 weapon (it seems that super stat does not scale ignition damage for some reason, while weapon stat does) stat that is ~43 less damage from ignition (both with radiant).
The good thing is that new damaging components and DoT ticks afterwards also apply scorch and it can ignite 2-3 times instead of 1. But if you are just shooting, you reapply ignition just in time and on damage phase this will still result in 8-12% less overall damage as it is not consistent. Sometimes it also makes 1k ignitions for larger damage in this scenario, but it is mostly random.
Not to mention, you can straight up use well of radiance that is needed anyway or use dawnblade as you already have dawn chorus

Also, particle reconstruction does not stack with other debuffs, so if there is a debuff in team, solar fulmination is better

Cull's is actually super good on Void/Prismatic Hunter. You can weaken with its melee out of invis, and since the crystals are coded as entities, sometimes if you create a weakening burst and then destroy a crystal, you can re-proc On the Prowl off the crystal. Weird interaction, not very consistent, but it's there. It also lets you just go ham by staying invis, weakening things, and wrecking them with the melee and healing the whole time. Not as op as the other classes for sure, but it's really good as a utility more than straight dps. I pair it with Celestial and use Tablet of Ruin for all the void/strand craziness as a good "all rounder" build I can leave on and take anywhere. But also, Kepri's Sting void build works too, shoot down a line of ads, create a bunch of crystals, let more pour in, dash through the enemies/crystals and you leave behind a trail of multiple explosions that absolutely wreck anything in the radius.

I think it would be cool if the new subclasses were based upon technology rather than simply raw light/dark powers:
1. Soulburn based upon Hive magic (quasi-darkness?) with that whole poison/plague vibe. Possibly even some Oryx/Taken abilities thrown in?
2. SIVA/Rasputin tech-based subclass from our own Golden Age tech (would it also be sorta Darkness due to Clovis?). I'm thinking Warmind Cell builds, but cranked up to be full subclasses combined with SIVA nanotech. Its underpinnings would be linked by the Traveler (Light), but it would feel uniquely "human" in its form.

Additional PvP-specific info:

Unbuffed, the two melee hit combo can't kill a player on it's own, unlike glaives past 180 melee. Even at 200 melee, players are left with a pixel of health if they don't have overshield, DR or any healing active. Having Necrotic Grip/Spirit of Necrotic doesn't improve melee TTK, but at max melee stat, allows you to run away after landing the second hit as the poison finishes them off. There's a huge delay after landing the first melee hit, making shooting or swapping afterward a risky play. I usually commit to the second hit, before following up with that hipfire spread to finish them off. Lastly, Soulfire Crystals can be destroyed by enemy players. This directly counters Soulfire Rancor's use as a trap.

35:06 Cross is speaking like a socialist here, where it's always great to please people with good intentions and free stuff when it's someone else's money...until it's his money. We'll see how his attitude changes with his game project where he'll have to manage costs and priorities... with his own money. He's comparing a much smaller company, Digital Exremes (DE), that has never done the numbers that Bungie has, the size of production and game scope...a boutique, if you will, albeit a sizable boutique. What each company releases is not comparable and he's played both so he knows the difference. It is an inconvenient truth.

If Cross wants Destiny 2 to continue, he should reframe the scope and set everyone's expectations in terms of deliveries. What can Bungie deliver that the community will pay for that is "profitable?" The reason why Bungie dev cost is SO much is how their game dev process is run. It runs like a AAA studio, which means they have very specialized ppl, doing very specific roles. That tends to mean you need to have more ppl and bigger teams or groups working together, it allows for higher quality and complex games but also slower development which all adds up higher cost. Smaller studios you have devs that do more than one thing, so you tend to have smaller teams, faster development but not as quality or complex games. I worked on several live services games in my career and most do not run like an AAA studio, so Bungie was the top of the line that many saw as the pinnacle of a live service game studio. Something I don’t think we will ever really see again, certainly not with the associated cost.

When I read the armor set skills I got curious and farmed for it. 
Currently running Stasis Warlock w/ Rime-Coat Raiment (200 Grenade stat and 150 Super stat)
 - Throw a enhance Stacey Turret at boss feet
 - Pull out THE SLAMMER (perks: Collective Demolitionist / Cold Steel)
-  When you're doing heavy attack, the sword will break Stasis Crystals thus procing Warmind Cells that damage and heal you, in addition to stasis shards that heal and provide class energy due to artifact.
 - Frost Armor keeps you alive, Shards heal you, rift keeps heals constant. 
 -  Collective Demolitionist provides grenade energy on every hit, including ALL hits from heavy sword hits. Provides endless Stacey Turrets and stasis shards. 
 - Also run Stasis Stasis Surge x2 for increase damage and Elemental Charge to keep up the activation of surges.

I have a Stasis Hunter using Mask of Fealty and I only need 1 more piece of Seven Seraph to complete the prophecy and become the legendary Super Saiyan.

Seriously, it is crazy good. Ager's Scepter (with catalyst, lets you juice the weapon with super energy for high damage, an extended battery, and it slows/freezes/shatters by just holding it on a target - which produce Stasis shards for frost armor and heals even from bosses)

Melee charges are on tap because of your dodge and the absurd amount of Stasis shards floating around.

Fragment for increased grenade cooldown after shattering a crystal gets the Shatter Grenade back on a very short cooldown.

I use the slowing dodge (mainly for the 3 fragment slots). Ager's releases a slowing burst, my dodge slows, my melee does every Stasis verb, and Shatter Grenade makes all those things go boom for burst damage.

So you are walking around with a Freeze Ray (not difficult to use as a primary in most content), Max stacks of Frost Armor (and heals from shards), and all of your abilities actively support the gameplay loop when used.

It may have become forgotten game history at this point, but Bungie was originally a Macintosh developer and Halo was first announced for the platform at the Macworld Expo by Steve Jobs himself. The studio sold out to Microsoft and turned Halo into a console exclusive for their new Xbox system. They've never been a trustworthy studio.

Bungie upped the price, separated content like the season pass & season with no raid and gave us Dungeons instead with keys being another added cost. Plus reduced Bright Dust drops, after that came the Portal. D2 became too pricey for old content. Greed killed D2.

To be honest, Halo’s relationship with Bungie nowadays is pretty disconnected. Almost of the original creators who made the studio(and made Destiny itself) have quit working there, or have been fired/laid off. Don’t say this to discredit the people who have been at the studio during the lifespan of Destiny at all. I’m just pointing out the fact that when Bungie partnered with Activison, the core group that made the place were practically gutted out of the studio. The dev team that we’ve had since is flat out a completely different group of visionaries. They are not the people who made Halo what it was back then.

I remember a scrappy little studio who wanted to use FPS to tell a meaningful story, a group of devs who wanted to bring FPS to consoles and make it actually feel good on a controller. I remember a group of devs who sold off their most well known franchise to get away from corporate influence. Devs who somehow managed to make a franchise that went beyond anything we could have anticipated while all the while keeping what made Halo great. Devs who had an ambitious narrative they wanted to tell over a decade. They used to have passion and wanted to create art. Now they are soulless and want to create money. R.I.P. Bungie, I will never buy or play another one of your games no matter how good it looks because no matter how it begins I know exactly how it will end.

When a company like Bungie suddenly brings in $500 million during the first week of a game's release, it can create a dangerous sense that this level of success will continue indefinitely. Instead of treating the windfall as an exceptional event, leadership may begin planning budgets around those unusually high revenues. That can lead to aggressive expansion, costly new projects, acquisitions, higher executive compensation, and rapid hiring that become difficult to sustain if future releases don't perform as well. When expectations outpace reality, the company can find itself burdened with expenses that were affordable during a blockbuster launch but become a serious financial strain once sales return to more typical levels. Sustainable growth requires recognizing that a huge hit is often the exception rather than the baseline.

The numbers, Mason.

Why Destiny 2 actually died: a labeled timeline, not a conspiracy.

Everyone's debating whether Sony "killed" Destiny 2 out of spite or pure financial math. Both miss it. This was mismanaged from the start. Here's every major incident, dated, in order.

[2000] Microsoft acquires Bungie. Halo becomes the studio's identity.

[2007] Bungie splits from Microsoft to stay independent. Microsoft keeps the Halo IP. First independence, won.

[2010] Bungie signs with Activision specifically because Activision lets them keep the new Destiny IP.

[2013] A year before launch, Bungie scraps the entire Destiny story and rebuilds it in two weeks. Original narrative director Joe Staten leaves the project.

[2014] Longtime audio director Marty O'Donnell is fired. He wins two separate legal rulings against Bungie, including one finding they breached the duty of good faith by stripping his stock.

[2018] Bungie takes a $100 million investment from NetEase — an Eastern mobile gaming company — giving them a board seat and licensing rights inside the Destiny universe. This becomes Destiny: Rising, a mobile game NetEase can build however they want, outside Bungie's own creative control.

[2019] Bungie splits from Activision early, regaining full independence.

[2021] IGN publishes a major investigation: 26 employees on record, documenting racism, sexism, and crunch culture. CEO Pete Parsons publicly apologizes. The HR head resigns within the week, writing that the department had been giving "bad actors safe harbor."

[2022] Sony acquires Bungie for $3.6 billion — one year after that public apology.

[2023] First layoffs hit, around 100 staff (8% of the company).

[2024] Second layoffs hit, 220 staff (17%). The same window, it's revealed CEO Pete Parsons spent over $2.4 million on 25 classic cars between 2022 and 2024 — buying through the layoffs, not after them. Also in 2024, Marathon's creative director is fired following an internal misconduct investigation. He sues, calls it a sham.

[2025] Sony's own court filings describe "a pattern of misconduct." Still unresolved in court — both sides are making claims, no verdict yet. Parsons steps down as CEO after 23 years, "passing the torch."

[2026] Sony discloses roughly $765 million in cumulative losses on the Bungie deal. A third round of layoffs hits. Marathon launches to a fraction of its day-one numbers. Destiny 2 goes into maintenance mode after nearly a decade.

That's everything. Two decades of incidents, not one decision.

Here's my Sword Logic review of it:

A studio that won its independence twice should have known the philosophy they put in their own game. Only what's built to survive has the right to exist. Bungie never cut the weak parts — they licensed pieces of their own universe to NetEase to buy their way out of Activision instead. They got a clear warning in 2021 that something inside was rotten, apologized once, and never finished the job. Three years later, the same pattern came back on the one project meant to save them. The man at the top bought 25 cars while the people who built his game lost theirs, then walked away clean enough to "pass the torch."

Sony didn't invent that logic. They just used it — cut the weak structure, stop the bleeding, move on.

But here's where I think there's a better ending, and it's not Sword Logic, it's Bomb Logic — the controlled detonation that clears space for something new to grow.

Epic did this with Paragon in 2018. Instead of just killing it quietly, they released $12-17 million worth of game assets to the public for free on Unreal Engine. They let the community build on what they'd made. That decision is the entire reason games like Predecessor exist today — fan-made successors built on Epic's own foundation, kept alive by the people who actually loved it.

Sony and Bungie could do the same thing here. Over 325,000 people signed a petition in less than three weeks asking for more Destiny. That's not noise, that's a real number, and I'd bet a meaningful chunk of those people would pay a real monthly fee to keep something going — I know I would. If Sony truly doesn't see a path to make this profitable as a AAA live-service game anymore, the honorable move isn't just shutting the lights off. It's releasing what they can, the way Epic did, and letting the community that actually built this culture keep it alive in some form.

That's the real choice in front of them right now. Cut it down and walk away — or use Bomb Logic the right way, and let something survive the blast.🌎☮️✊🏿

Sony bought Bungie for 3.6 billion dollars. Bungie was also given a massive lump sum of cash from Sony immediately after the acquisition specifically for employee retention.
Many key employees were laid off anyways, and that money seemingly just disappeared (it's widely believed that most of it was seized by Pete Parsons to finance his vintage car collection).
The Bungie executive team then essentially worked a loophole in their contract with Sony to buy time until their stock vested (Bungie's leadership were allowed to operate independent of Sony's oversight so long as each quarter was more profitable than the previous. They achieved these consistent profits through even more layoffs.)
This cycle repeated for years, leading us to today. Sony has finally seized full control of what's left of Bungie, a studio that has bled almost all their major talent, and an executive team that has either  fled with their vested stock, or otherwise has been actively hostile to Sony's plans since day 1.
Yeah, if I were Sony, I'd just want to torpedo the studio and forget about it all too tbh.

I’ve been playing around with the threadling build for a couple days now, the one thing nobody seems to mention is how it literally breaks the game. Eventually, and I haven’t figured out exactly what triggers it but I think it has to do with picking up and throwing tangles, your rift will stop spawning threadlings on cast, your guns will go invisible and can’t be reloaded, and when gaining Woven Mail to reduce damage taken, it won’t actually reduce the damage despite having the buff. I also tried with Weave Walk and I got stuck in third person and couldn’t cast ANY abilities. Also when trying to emote you don’t leave first person and can’t see the emote, but others can. Fortunately dying or leaving/joining activities resets it until it breaks next, but it makes it hard to use in raids when there are limited revives, or people doing mechanics that split you up so nobody can revive you

Edit: Just got to 19:10 in the video and he broke just like I was saying. Evidently it’s not tangled since he didn’t even generate any. Will need to do some more testing, or if anyone knows what causes the problems to try to avoid it please let me know

Edit Part 2: After reading some comments and doing some testing myself, you can simply swap artifacts then back immediately to fix it when it bugs. No need for casting abilities, dying, leaving/rejoining or anything like that. Just open inventory, click another artifact, then click again to put back the Implement of Curiosity

The problem with subscriptions is that the price inevitably rises. And it will rise over, and over, and over again. We've seen it constantly. A subscription is NOT the way. You WILL end up paying far more for a year of content this way, than if you just bought it flat out.

I was never the strongest Warlock, and I never had one of those crazy meta builds that melted everything in seconds. But that never mattered to me. What mattered was the feeling Destiny gave me every time I logged in.

I wasn’t one of the Day One veterans from the original Destiny. I joined Destiny 1 much later, but I got Destiny 2 during its launch month and instantly fell in love with it. Every time I started the game, I felt at home. Whether I was exploring new destinations, chasing loot, or simply wandering around the Tower, Destiny always had a way of making my day a little better.

One of my biggest regrets is that I never completed a raid. I always worried about being a burden to the team or making mistakes. Looking back, I probably should have tried anyway. But even without raids, I still managed to create countless memories and enjoy every expansion, every season, and every adventure.

What truly made Destiny special wasn’t just the game itself it was the community. The discussions, the theories, the excitement before new releases, the shared victories and frustrations. For nearly a decade, Guardians from all over the world built something unique together.

Even though some DLCs have been controversial and their quality has sometimes been questioned, I still refuse to delete Destiny 2 from my PS5. No matter how much storage space I need, Destiny stays. There are games I enjoy, and then there’s Destiny.

I don’t know if we’ll ever get a Destiny 3. Maybe we will, maybe we won’t. But if one day Bungie calls us back for another journey, if one day we meet again among the stars of Destiny 3, then I’ll be there.

That FOMO that Aztec mentions is what ultimately killed it for me. I hated losing access to content I had paid for. I didn’t care about the artifacts. I did care about the story and lore that I couldn’t quite get through, not to mention there were really small windows for certain story moments, like the end of Season of Arrival, or the cinema scene where Uldren’s ghost finds him for the first time as well, etc. 

Anyway, it was incumbent on the devs to figure out a way to keep all of that content available. They could have worked with Sony and Xbox to find a way to allow players to download certain content as they needed, if they really wanted to keep the file size down, that way new players aren’t intimidated by the huge file size. It would be something they could manage IF they just wanted to try out Destiny 2, because diehard players who were already committed wouldn’t care about the file size.

I would love to see Bungie and Sony incentivized to find a way to unvault all of the content, as that is what killed the playerbase. It’s what killed it for me. Oh and adding a Destiny forge mode seemed like a no brainer as that would have easily helped keep PvP alive.

I remember the day I discovered Destiny. It was through the simple PS4 demo, where all you could play were a handful of missions (if I remember correctly). I would constantly create new characters just to experience those missions again and again. Then one day, I got Destiny along with all of its DLC for my birthday. At that point, I was still quite young, and after beating the campaigns, I would endlessly roam around Venus, watching fireteams tackle the Vault of Glass. I was never able to complete a raid myself. Nonetheless I would constantly roam around the unique worlds of Destiny completely encapsulated.

I remember the day after Destiny 2 launched. I was in the 6th grade and had preordered the game for my birthday. What I didn't know was that my mother planned to pull me out of school to pick up the game, along with a bunch of merch she had purchased for me at GameStop. To this day, I still have that merch. I have practically grown up with Destiny 2, as I am now approaching 20 years old. Despite my love-hate relationship with the game and all the breaks I've taken, I have always come back. I stopped playing before Lightfall came out and have only recently returned to reignite my passion and love for this game. This game holds a very special place in my heart, and I know it does for countless others as well. This game was my beacon during COVID. All I remember doing was dodging classroom zoom calls to grind GOS during Divinities questline. What great times...

Remember, my fellow Guardians,
Rage, rage against the dying of the light!

In the hollow halls of outpost might,
A Rook roams through endless night.
Shadows whisper where data ghosts sleep,
He claims what the fallen failed to keep.
Gold glimmers in the ruin’s dust,
A relic’s shine, a gleam of trust.
Circuits hum, the air turns thin,

Just echoes of battles long ago.
Yet every scrap, each golden find,
Builds his legend — piece by grind.

Tip - If you find a gold "gun", just take the mod off and leave the gun. Its the difference between 1 inventory slot and 8 inventory slots. 

If your inventory has atleast 1 open slot, equip the gun, strip the mod off then drop the gun and pick yours back up. 

If you have 9 or more inventory slots open, equip the gun, and your gun will go to backpack with 1 or more slots open. Stip the mod, drop the gun, equip your gun again.

This is best for common guns and ones sold in the armory. More rare to find guns, just take the whole thing.

My friends and I all walk different paths now. We still had one last hurrah where they all came back for final shape to beat the witness. I had a run last night where me and my 2 friends were doing the Traxus priority contracts on Perimeter and we got third partied after we wiped a different team. We fought it out for a bit but ultimately my two friends went down and I ran away in the hopes to revive them because I was Triage. I hid for about 3 minutes waiting for my ult to charge and was able to pick them both up. We immediately dipped because my friends had been looted but each still had a single gun and a bit of ammo. 

We headed to North Relay to do some quick looting and then extract, but while we were there we found a person just stood still looting in a building. We coordinated an attack on them and downed them before they had a chance to move. Their teammates rolled in but we were able to win the 3v2. Crazy thing happened... while looting them I realized one of the items in their bag said that it was labelled as a "returned item" and would be returned to my friend after extract. I asked him to look at the gun and sure enough it was HIS gun that the team that third partied us looted off of him. We ended up going in with green items, almost getting wiped, and then somehow killing the team that had originally killed us, and getting out with blue shields, blue backpacks, some blue/purple guns and mods, and TONS of meds.

We ended up losing that kit after about 2 hours of runs and I was kinda bummed but all it takes is wiping one kitted squad and you're right back to where you were. Also, while doing those 2 hours of runs with that blue/purple kit, we were able to get a good amount of blue loot to throw in the vault for a rainy day fund.
People remember losing something they value more than getting something they like.

I can guarantee that whatever metrics Sony had for this game, it is *not* hitting numbers just shy of 90k on PC. So unless console has double, or triple that count, this is considered a win by not being disqualified from the race. For whatever that's worth. 

And just from a financial standpoint, even assuming the "triple digits" pipe dream, that's still only $12 million from sales alone. And estimates report the game cost $250 million to make? And Sony dumped $3.6 Billion into Bungie? That is...oof.

The game is not a success as a AAA company. As a side project? It’s good. Trying to move on to a new IP with a niche genre with a competitor that has 2 or 3x its player numbers is very bad in terms of how bad Bungie has been in the past few years. Destiny was successful because it had its casual aspects and hardcore aspects in both PvE and PvP, it was a game made for EVERYONE. This isn’t. 

They tried to make a game for the more sweaty players, which let’s be honest, at least 1 in 10 gamers are a sweatlord and the rest are more of your average Joe. Making a game for that 10% of people in a community is amazing for those type of players, but it’s not attractive to your more chill players. It’s just meant to lose money, no matter what.
